title: Phishing simulation efficacy — click-rate trend and repeat-clicker detection
exclusions: Does NOT deliver remediation content directly; feeds HR / L&D for follow-up.
evidence: Monthly 'phishing_sim_efficacy' writes click-rate trend and repeat-clicker lists to compliance_summary.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Art.21(2)(g) — beyond completion, regulators want to see the program is effective.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIS2 Art.21(2)(g) (Cyber-hygiene and training) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.46.2: Phishing simulation efficacy — click-rate trend and repeat-clicker detection.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.46.2' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

compliance.assurance_rationale: PCI 12.6 effectiveness via knowledge retention — simulation click rate is an accepted metric.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that PCI DSS 12.6 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.46.2: Phishing simulation efficacy — click-rate trend and repeat-clicker detection.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.46.2' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: When the 90-day rolling click-rate rises above the programme baseline by +3 percentage points, or when an employee clicks on 3+ simulations in 180 days, the UC fires.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Click-rates within baseline and employees with ≤2 clicks do NOT fire.

dataSources: See subcategory preamble. — supplemented by authentication logs, system audit trails, and configuration management database (CMDB) asset inventory exports
spl:
index=phishing sourcetype=knowbe4:campaign OR sourcetype=proofpoint:psa earliest=-180d
| bin _time span=30d
| stats count(eval(action="clicked")) as clicks count as total by _time
| eval click_rate=round(clicks/total*100,2)
| stats last(click_rate) as current avg(click_rate) as baseline
| eval drift=current-baseline
| where drift>3
| appendpipe [search index=phishing action=clicked earliest=-180d | stats dc(campaign_id) as clicks by employee | where clicks>=3]
description: Tracks efficacy: the programme is green only if click-rate stays within baseline and repeat-clickers are followed up. Drift above threshold is a programme-level finding.
value: Turns the awareness programme into a measurable control, not an annual training badge.
implementation: (1) Ingest campaign events from the phishing-sim platform; (2) compute baselines per quarter; (3) run monthly; (4) deliver repeat-clicker lists to HR for targeted training. Indexes required: `index=phishing`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=knowbe4:campaign`, `sourcetype=proofpoint:psa`.
visualization: Line chart of click rate over time with baseline band, table of repeat clickers, single-value 'current click rate vs baseline'.
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Authorised compliance operations** — The compliance and governance team performs routine control testing, evidence gathering, and policy reviews that generate legitimate activity matching this use case's detection patterns. These operations are documented in the compliance programme calendar and should be excluded by the compliance-ops service account tag.

2. **Scheduled system maintenance windows** — Approved maintenance activities including patching, upgrades, and configuration changes produce expected control deviations during documented change windows that are pre-approved through the change advisory board (CAB).

3. **External audit and assessment activities** — During periodic audits (internal or external), assessors perform sampling, testing, and evidence collection activities that generate unusual access patterns covered by the engagement letter and audit notification.

4. **Third-party service provider operations** — Contracted service providers performing legitimate operations under signed agreements generate expected activity that may trigger monitoring rules during normal service delivery windows.

**Suppression mechanism:** Maintain a `compliance_exception_register` KV Store lookup containing approved maintenance windows, audit engagement periods, service provider identifiers, and compliance team service accounts. The correlation search joins this lookup and suppresses matches where `exception_status=approved` and the exception remains within its validity period.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Indexes**: `phishing` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Search-head capacity**: Subsearch-based detections need `subsearch_maxtime` ≥ 60s and `subsearch_maxout` ≥ 50000 in `limits.conf`. For high-volume environments, tune `[search] max_searches_per_cpu` up from default.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `Art.21(2)(g)`. The control objective is: "Evidence that NIS2 Art.21(2)(g) (Cyber-hygiene and training) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.46.2: Phishing simulation efficacy — click-rate trend and repeat-clicker detection." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into `phishing` under `knowbe4:campaign`, `proofpoint:psa`, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


Four distinct operational considerations shape this detection's rollout:


• **Hint 1:** Ingest campaign events from the phishing-sim platform. Ingestion SLA drift is usually invisible until it breaks something — monitor the feed's freshness with a companion search that alerts when the newest event is older than the expected cadence.

• **Hint 2:** compute baselines per quarter. The register referenced here is primary evidence — treat changes to it with the same discipline as code changes.

• **Hint 3:** run monthly. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** deliver repeat-clicker lists to HR for targeted training. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

The detection fires when `drift > 3`. For "Phishing simulation efficacy — click-rate trend and repeat-clicker detection," the aggregation exposes temporal structure that a single point-in-time reading cannot — the compliance question is about behaviour over a period, not at an instant. This threshold is the operational heart of the control — set it too low and the signal drowns in noise; set it too high and real compliance deviations go undetected. The value `3` should never be treated as universal; it must be calibrated against your specific operational baseline.

Calibration procedure: before enabling this as a production alert, run the base search over the last 30 days with the threshold removed and compute distribution statistics:

```spl
<base search> earliest=-30d
| stats count by drift
| stats avg(drift) as mean, perc50(drift) as p50, perc90(drift) as p90, perc99(drift) as p99, max(drift) as hi
```

Set the production threshold between p90 and p99 depending on your organisation's alert fatigue tolerance. Document the chosen value in the control specification alongside the date of calibration and the sample size — auditors will ask how you arrived at `3` and the defensible answer is 'data, plus a documented review by the control owner,' not 'the template said so.'

The `count()` aggregation over the last 180 days is a deliberate choice. Compliance reports on sustained behaviour, not instantaneous spikes — a single noisy event crossing the threshold for one second should not page the on-call. If your organisation requires point-in-time sensitivity (e.g. real-time fraud detection), shorten the time window and aggregation span; for accountability reporting, widen them. Tune for the regulation's actual expectation, not engineering preference.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.46.2: Phishing simulation efficacy — click-rate trend and repeat-c]
cron_schedule = 0 */6 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -180d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.46.2,reg=NIS2
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=phishing sourcetype=knowbe4:campaign earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `_time`, `baseline`, `click_rate`, `clicks`, `current`, `drift`, `employee`, `total`. Run:

```spl
index=phishing sourcetype=knowbe4:campaign earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table _time, baseline, click_rate, clicks, current, drift, employee, total
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(d) Threshold calibration:** Before enabling as a production alert, run the base search with the threshold removed and profile the distribution of `drift`:

```spl
<base search> earliest=-30d
| stats perc50(drift) as p50 perc90(drift) as p90 perc99(drift) as p99 max(drift) as hi
```

The current threshold `3` should sit between p90 and p99 for a balanced signal-to-noise ratio. If it's below p50, expect heavy alert volume; if above p99, expect under-detection.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.46.2: NIS2 evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Line Chart Of Click Rate Over Time With Baseline Band (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 2 — Table Of Repeat Clickers (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 3 — Single-Value 'Current Click Rate Vs Baseline' (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Too many or too few alerts:** The threshold is almost certainly miscalibrated for your estate — re-run the percentile calibration (Step 3d) on a representative 30-day window and pick a value between p90 and p99.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**NIS2-specific failure modes:**

• 24/7 reporting obligation (Art. 23): NIS2 requires early warning within 24 hours of awareness of a significant incident. Detections that back this SLA must compute elapsed time from the SOC's first-acknowledged timestamp (not the resolve-time) and must trigger independently of business hours — the regulatory clock does not pause overnight.

• Member-state variation: NIS2 is transposed individually by each EU Member State with some latitude on thresholds and sector scoping. A detection correct for German IT-SiG 3 (BSI) may be misaligned with Dutch CWV or French LPM. Use `nis2_member_state_config.csv` to parametrize thresholds per jurisdiction; never hardcode.

• Essential vs Important entity criteria: the applicable obligations depend on sector + size. Before alerting, verify the monitored entity's designation; a detection firing on an out-of-scope subsidiary causes unnecessary regulatory notifications that erode trust with the national CSIRT.

• Supply-chain (Art. 21(2)(d)) extent: NIS2 explicitly includes direct suppliers' cybersecurity posture. Detections must cover monitored-by-you-not-owned-by-you infrastructure (outsourced SaaS, managed SIEM tenants) — these are in scope even if operationally run by a third party. BA/BAA-style governance applies.
grandmaExplanation: We help you see who still needs training so awareness stays current across teams and roles.
